text: Upon inserting the scope, a large inflammatory mass is visible in the right iliac fossa, involving the thickened cecum, appendices epiploicae, small bowel, and cecum base. The surgeon performs meticulous adhesiolysis to identify the appendix within this mass.

Upon inserting the scope, the surgeon observes a large inflammatory mass in the right iliac fossa, consisting of the thickened and inflamed cecum, appendices epiploicae, small bowel, and cecum base. Meticulous adhesiolysis is performed to identify the appendix within this mass.

The surgical team orients themselves to the anatomy, noting the unusually firm cecum due to localized peritonitis and inflammatory changes. Gradually, the tip of the appendix becomes visible, aiding in navigation.

The surgeon navigates the inflamed anatomical structures, focusing on the firm cecum and surrounding tissues affected by localized peritonitis. Gradually, the tip of the appendix becomes visible, aiding in the orientation and progression of the appendectomy.
The surgeon observes peritoneal flakes in the abdominal cavity, indicating a contained seal perforation, which explains the absence of generalized peritonitis signs in the patient.
The surgeon observes peritoneal flakes in the surgical field, indicating a contained seal perforation of the appendix, which explains the absence of generalized peritonitis signs in the patient.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection using a blunt grasper or suction tip, navigating through thickened inflamed tissues. The tissues are visibly difficult to handle due to inflammation, and minimal handling is advised to prevent excessive oozing and maintain a clear surgical field.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection using a blunt grasper or suction tip, navigating through thickened inflamed tissues. The tissues are visibly difficult to handle due to inflammation, and minimal handling is advised to prevent excessive oozing and maintain a clear surgical field.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions from the appendix epiploicae, which have become fibrinolytic and thick. Meticulous dissection reveals pus, indicating a perforation likely at the tip or distal one-third of the appendix.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions around the appendix epiploicae, which have become fibrinolytic and thickened. Meticulous dissection in the correct plane allows for the peeling off of these adhesions, revealing underlying structures more clearly. Pus is visible, indicating a perforation likely located at the tip or distal one-third of the appendix.

The surgeon observes a perforation at the base of the appendix, with the surrounding cecum appearing gangrenous and sloughed out. The entire base of the appendix is visibly affected, lacking a viable stump for an endo-loop application. The surgical field shows extensive tissue damage, requiring careful dissection to avoid further trauma.

The surgeon observes a perforation at the base of the appendix, with the surrounding cecum appearing gangrenous and sloughed out. The entire base of the appendix is visibly affected, requiring careful dissection to avoid trauma to the tissues.

The surgeon exposes the area of perforation and sloughed tissue at the cecum, extending to the cecal wall. Applying pressure reveals additional pus emerging from the perforated site, indicating active perforation.

The surgeon exposes the area of perforation and sloughed tissue at the cecum, noting its extension to the cecal wall. Upon applying pressure to the cecum, additional pus is observed emerging from the perforated area, indicating an active perforation.

The surgeon mobilizes the cecum on the inferior wall region, assessing the friable and inflamed tissue to determine the best repair approach. Conventional suturing is considered due to the tissue's condition, avoiding the use of an endostapler.

The surgeon mobilizes the cecum on the inferior wall region, assessing the friable and inflamed tissue to determine the best approach for repair. Conventional suturing is considered due to the condition of the tissue, avoiding the use of an endostapler.

The surgeon uses suction to remove sloughed tissue, carefully manipulating and identifying the extent of diseased tissue for excision. The remnant tissue is assessed for healthiness to determine suitability for suturing. The surgical field reveals a large perforation between the cecum and the inferior mesentery.

The surgeon uses suction to remove sloughed tissue, carefully manipulating and identifying the extent of diseased tissue for excision. The surgical field reveals a large perforation between the cecum and the inferior mesentery, with visible tissue damage likely exacerbated by the patient's diabetic condition.

The video shows the appendix with sloughed-out tissue forming a phlegmon or mass. Thick, fibrotic adhesions are visible attaching to the abdominal wall. The surgeon uses gauze to manage oozing and improve visibility by preventing blood from staining the tissue and darkening the image.

The surgeon observes the sloughed-out tissue forming a phlegmon-like mass in the appendix, with thick, fibrotic adhesions to the abdominal wall. Gauze is used to manage oozing and improve visibility by preventing blood from staining the tissue and darkening the image.

The surgeon mobilizes the cecum further, applying traction to safely handle the tissue. The avascular planes are identified, following the plane between the mesocolon and Gerota's fascia, as well as the retroperitoneal tissues and their covering fascia.

The surgeon mobilizes the cecum further, applying traction to safely handle the tissue. They identify and adhere to the avascular planes between the mesocolon, Gerota's fascia, and retroperitoneal tissues, similar to the approach used in right hemicolectomy for malignancies.
The surgeon uses gauze to mop small oozes, brightening the surgical field. Sharp dissection is performed gradually, separating small tissue sections with precision.
The surgeon uses gauze to mop up small oozes, brightening the surgical field. Sharp dissection is performed gradually, separating small tissue segments with precision.

The surgeon carefully dissects the inflamed tissue in small sections, avoiding large blocks, to minimize bleeding. Small thrombosed vessels are present due to infection, and an ultrasonic shear is used to control oozing and bleeding during the dissection.
The surgeon carefully dissects small tissue segments using ultrasonic shears to minimize bleeding and oozing in the inflamed and infected appendiceal region, where vessels are small and thrombosed.

The surgeon gently removes sloughed tissue to identify the exact area for incision. The appendix is incised while ensuring proximity to the mesentery, avoiding retroperitoneal structures like the ureters. Care is taken to prevent injury to vessels in the mesentery near the small bowel.

The surgeon gently removes the sloughed out tissue to identify the exact area of the appendix. An incision is made into the remaining tissue of the appendix, taking care to avoid extending too retroperitoneally due to the proximity of the ureters. The dissection is performed close to the mesentery, ensuring the appendix is kept flush to avoid injury to the vessels supplying the small bowel.

The surgeon continues the dissection, identifying and separating tissue planes around the appendix. The appendicular artery is visualized, and a bipolar device is used to coagulate the vessel, minimizing bleeding.

The surgeon continues the dissection step by step, identifying and separating the tissue planes around the appendix. The appendicular artery is visible, and a bipolar device is used to coagulate the vessel, minimizing bleeding as the appendix is freed from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to coagulate a vessel, achieving hemostasis and controlling bleeding effectively. The surgical field is clear, allowing for precise dissection.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to coagulate a vessel, achieving good hemostasis and controlling bleeding effectively. The surgical field is preserved, allowing for precise dissection.

The surgeon uses gauze to mop up the area, absorbing fluids without traumatizing the inflamed tissue. The gauze provides traction and retraction, maintaining a clean surgical field while preventing frequent escape of pneumo.

The surgeon uses gauze to mop up the area, absorbing fluids without traumatizing the inflamed tissue. The gauze provides traction and retraction, maintaining tissue color and clarity for identifying anatomical planes.

The surgeon manipulates the phlegmon mass using assisting hands, while maintaining the original tissue color to identify the correct planes. An optical trocar is placed through a ten millimeter port at the umbilicus, with additional working ports at the suprapubic level and right lumbar region, providing adequate space and angles for manipulation and suturing. Tissue removal continues near the appendix.

The surgeon manipulates the phlegmon mass using assisting hands, with an optical trocar placed in the umbilicus and working ports at the suprapubic level and right lumbar region. The surgeon removes tissues near the appendix, utilizing the ports for adequate working space and angle.

The surgeon meticulously releases the appendix, taking care to avoid thermal injury to the cecal wall. Fatty tissues are gradually removed, and a bipolar instrument is used to coagulate any bleeding areas, ensuring precise control over the fibrotic adhesions.

The surgeon meticulously releases the appendix, taking care to avoid thermal injury to the cecal wall. Fatty tissues are gradually removed, and a bipolar device is used to coagulate any bleeding areas, ensuring precise dissection and minimizing thermal damage.

The surgeon lyses adhesions to free the appendix, preparing for an appendectomy. The appendix is mostly freed with minimal adherent tissue remaining. The dissection is performed in a cylindrical manner around the appendix. Smoke is released through an accessory port, and the telescope is reinserted into the trocar for a clear view.

The surgeon has lysed adhesions to free the appendix, preparing for appendectomy. The appendix is mostly freed with minimal adherent tissue remaining, which is being carefully dissected. The dissection is performed in a cylindrical manner around the appendix, ensuring thorough separation from surrounding tissues. Smoke is released through an accessory port to maintain a clear view.

The surgeon inserts the telescope into the trocar to ensure a clear view. A small fatty strand is visible between the appendix, and the surgeon uses sharp dissection to divide it at an appropriate level.

The surgeon uses sharp dissection to divide a fatty strand between the appendix and surrounding tissue. The base of the appendix is visible at the junction with the cecum, revealing a gangrenous portion and a perforated area.
The base of the appendix is visible at the junction with the cecum, showing a gangrenous and perforated area. Small tissue strands are being lysed to isolate the appendicular attachment for removal.

The surgeon lyses small strands of tissue to isolate the appendicular attachment. Traction is applied to visualize the tissues, followed by an incision into the sloughed-off tissue at the cecum level. Care is taken not to incise too close to the cecal wall, allowing for reassessment of slough, infection, and unhealthy tissue in the cecum.

The surgeon applies traction to expose the tissues at the cecum level, incising the sloughed off tissue while avoiding proximity to the cecal wall. The incision aims to divide the appendix and reassess the extent of slough and infection in the cecum.

The surgeon uses ultrasonic shears to divide the appendix completely, ensuring minimal excision of unhealthy sloughed tissue in the cecum. Traction is adjusted to improve exposure and view of the tissues, preparing for sutured closure to prevent fistula formation or leaks.

The surgeon uses ultrasonic shears to divide the appendix completely, while minimizing the excision of unhealthy tissue in the cecum. The sloughed and unhealthy tissue is visible, and traction is adjusted to improve exposure and view of the tissues.
The surgeon prepares for sutured closure of the appendix stump, avoiding the use of an endo loop due to the risk of fistula formation or leakage from the cecum.

After excising the appendix, the surgeon uses a suction and a dramatic instrument to assess the depth of sloughing in the cecum, revealing a tunneled pattern. The surrounding cecal wall appears healthy, and the surgeon identifies the extent of sloughed and unhealthy tissue to determine the level of vascularity for closure.

After excising the appendix, the surgeon uses a suction and a dramatic instrument to assess the level of sloughing, which extends deeper into the cecum in a tunneled fashion. The surrounding cecal wall appears healthy, and the surgeon carefully removes the slough to evaluate the vascularity and healthiness of the tissue for closure.

The surgeon uses a suction instrument to remove pus from the sloughed and unhealthy tissue at the junction of the appendix and cecum. The suction instrument freely enters the cecum, indicating the presence of pus collected due to tissue sloughing. The surgeon assesses the extent of damage in the cecum and at the appendix-cecum junction.

The surgeon uses a suction instrument to remove pus from the stump area, revealing an opening into the cecum. The suction instrument freely enters the cecum, extracting pus likely accumulated due to sloughing and unhealthy tissue. The surgeon assesses whether the damage extends further into the cecum or remains localized at the junction of the appendix and cecum.

The surgeon uses suction to remove pus from the cecum and applies traction to identify the level of tissue damage. Healthy tissue is visible, and the remaining sloughed and gangrenous tissue is excised. The edges are refashioned to ensure only healthy tissue remains.

The surgeon uses suction to remove pus from the cecum and applies traction to identify the extent of tissue damage. Healthy tissue is visible, and the remaining sloughed and gangrenous tissue is excised. The edges are refashioned to ensure only healthy tissue remains.

The surgeon uses ultrasonic shears to excise the last of the sloughed tissue, ensuring it is kept with the specimen to prevent infection. The opening into the cecum is now clearer, revealing a healthy cecal wall. Suction is used to remove any remaining collection from the cecum, confirming the surrounding tissues are healthy.

The surgeon uses ultrasonic shears to excise the last of the sloughed tissue, carefully fashioning the edges to ensure hemostasis. The excised tissue is kept with the specimen to prevent any small bits from being lost in the abdominal cavity, which could lead to infection.

The surgeon observes a clearer opening into the cecum, noting that the cecal wall appears healthy. The collection within the cecum is being suctioned out, ensuring that the surrounding tissues are healthy. The previously sloughed tissue has been completely removed, revealing intact walls.

The surgeon has removed all sloughed tissue, revealing healthy cecal walls with good vascularity. The suction apparatus is palpated at the small opening into the cecum, confirming the removal of unhealthy tissue and collected fluid. The cecum is mobilized inferiorly for comprehensive suturing using 2-0 Vicryl to close the opening.

The surgeon palpates the tip of the suction apparatus, confirming the small opening into the cecum. The surrounding tissue appears healthy with good vascularity. The surgeon uses suction to remove collected fluid and pus, ensuring clean edges. A 2-0 Vicryl suture is used to close the opening in the cecum, with the cecum mobilized inferiorly for comprehensive suturing.

The surgeon guides the needle into the opening of the cecum, taking thick bites for interrupted sutures in the inflamed, friable tissue. The needle, approximately 29 mm in size, is introduced through a 10 mm port. The second posterior layer is included in the suturing, ensuring precise placement within the lumen before knotting the sutures in a conventional laparoscopic manner.

The surgeon guides the needle into the opening of the cecum, taking thick bites for interrupted sutures in the inflamed, friable tissue. The needle, approximately 29 mm in size, is introduced through a 10 mm port. The second posterior layer is included in the suturing, ensuring secure closure through the lumen.

The surgeon precisely places the suture at the desired location and performs conventional laparoscopic knotting, starting with a surgeon's throw followed by single throws. The tissues are tightened adequately to approximate them without excessive pressure, preventing cut-through. The approximation results in a visible dumbbell formation of the tissues.

The surgeon performs a surgeon's throw followed by single throws to approximate the tissues without excessive pressure, ensuring they come together for healing. The approximation results in a visible dumbbell formation of the tissues.

The surgeon takes closely spaced suture bites to achieve a watertight closure of the cecum, preventing spillage of bowel contents into the abdominal cavity. The suture limbs are kept small, with one limb slightly longer for easier manipulation during the suturing process. The second suture bite is being placed.

The surgeon takes closely spaced suture bites to achieve a watertight closure, preventing spillage of contents from the small bowel and cecum into the abdominal cavity. The limbs of the sutures are kept small, with one limb slightly longer for easier manipulation during suturing.

The surgeon takes a full-thickness bite at the small opening in the cecum, carefully controlling the needle's tip to achieve precise exit points. The curvature of the needle is utilized to minimize tissue trauma during removal.

The surgeon takes a full-thickness bite at the small opening, carefully controlling the needle's tip to achieve the desired exit point. The curvature of the needle is used to minimize tissue trauma during removal.

The surgeon tightens the knot, approximating the tissues for effective closure. The third bite is taken, controlling the needle's exit point to ensure precise placement at the desired level, completing the closure of the opening.

The surgeon tightens the knot to approximate the tissues, ensuring a secure closure. The third bite is taken, controlling the needle's exit point to achieve precise placement at the desired level, marking the end of the opening closure.

The surgeon removes the needle and performs a double throw surgeon's knot followed by single throws to secure the suture. The knot is tightened adequately to approximate the tissues without cutting through, promoting effective wound healing.

The surgeon removes the needle and performs knotting using a double throw followed by single throws to secure the suture. The knot is tightened adequately to approximate the tissues without cutting through, promoting effective wound healing.

The surgeon ties the last knot of the stitch, maintaining a clean surgical field. The omentum is identified and pulled close to the cecum. A seromuscular bite is taken on the cecal wall at the edge of the suture line, followed by a bite in the omentum to perform a pexy, similar to a duodenal perforation repair.

The surgeon ties the last knot for the current stitch, maintaining a clean surgical field. The omentum is identified and pulled close to the cecum. A seromuscular bite is taken on the cecal wall at the edge of the suture line, followed by a bite in the omentum to perform a pexy.

The surgeon tightens the sutures to secure the omentum closely to the suture line, ensuring it remains in place to help control potential leakage and localize any peritonitis. This technique aids in preventing generalized peritonitis by promoting adherence of the omentum to the suture line.

The surgeon tightens the sutures to secure the omentum in place over the suture line, ensuring it adheres closely to minimize potential leakage and localize any peritonitis. This technique helps prevent generalized peritonitis by keeping the omentum stable rather than loosely draped.

The surgeon places additional omentum into the right iliac fossa to help control inflammation. The small bowel is examined from the terminal ileocecal junction to check for the presence of Meckel's diverticulum.

The surgeon places additional omentum into the right iliac fossa to help control the inflammatory process. The small bowel is examined from the terminal ileocecal junction to check for the presence of Meckel's diverticulum.

The surgeon introduces an endobag into the abdomen, displaying its two open limbs. The dissected portion of the appendix is placed into the endobag and retrieved in a piecemeal manner to prevent spillage. Additional omentum is pulled into the right iliac fossa, and a drain is placed in the right iliac fossa and pelvis. The procedure concludes with the closure of the ports using sutures.

The surgeon introduces an endobag into the abdomen, displaying its two open limbs. The dissected appendix is placed into the endobag and retrieved in a piecemeal manner to prevent spillage. Additional omentum is placed in the right iliac fossa, and a drain is positioned in both the right iliac fossa and the pelvis. The procedure concludes with the closure of the ports using sutures.
